title: Home Electricity Cost Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Your Home's Monthly Electricity Costs
description: Calculate kWh usage and monthly electricity costs for household appliances. Free, fast & accurate. 2026 rates.
intro: This calculator tells you exactly how much any electrical device costs to run each month — and over a full year. It uses three inputs — the device's power draw in Watts, the number of hours per day it runs, and your local electricity rate in $/kWh — to compute real energy consumption and dollar cost. The core formula is: **kWh/month = (Watts × Hours/day × 30) ÷ 1,000**, then **Monthly Cost = kWh/month × $/kWh**. Use it whenever you want to compare appliances, audit your utility bill, or decide whether upgrading to an energy-efficient model actually pays off. According to the U.S. Energy Information Administration, the average American household consumed 899 kWh/month in 2022 at an average retail rate of 16.21 ¢/kWh — meaning the typical monthly bill runs roughly $145. Knowing which devices drive that number gives you actionable control over it.
keyTakeaway: **Monthly Cost ($) = (Watts × Hours/day × 30 ÷ 1,000) × Rate ($/kWh)** — e.g., a 1,500 W space heater running 6 h/day at $0.16/kWh costs **$43.20/month** ($518.40/year).
useCases: Comparing a 55-inch LED TV (120 W) vs. an old plasma (380 W) to calculate the annual savings from upgrading — roughly $39/year at the national average rate.
Estimating the monthly cost of running a central air conditioner (3,500 W, ~8 h/day in summer) before the season starts to budget utility bills accurately.
Calculating whether a smart power strip or unplugging a gaming console (standby draw: 10–15 W, 24 h/day) actually saves a meaningful amount — about $1.73–$2.59/month.
Evaluating the payback period on an ENERGY STAR refrigerator (400 W → 150 W) to decide if the upfront cost is worth the ~$24/year in electricity savings.
Auditing home office energy use (desktop PC + monitors + printer) to identify the biggest cost driver and whether switching to a laptop setup cuts the bill significantly.
Preparing for an EV home charger installation by estimating the added monthly electricity cost of charging a Level 2 EVSE (7.2 kW × ~2 h/day) before calling an electrician.

fields.label: Hours per Day
fields.help: Average hours the appliance runs daily.

explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The math behind electricity billing is straightforward once you understand the unit conversion between Watts and kilowatt-hours.

```
Step 1 — Daily energy (kWh/day):
  kWh/day = (Watts × Hours per Day) ÷ 1,000

Step 2 — Monthly energy (kWh/month):
  kWh/month = kWh/day × 30

Step 3 — Monthly cost ($):
  Monthly Cost = kWh/month × Rate ($/kWh)

Step 4 — Annual cost ($):
  Annual Cost = Monthly Cost × 12

────────────────────────────────
Example: 100 W bulb × 8 h/day
  kWh/day  = (100 × 8) ÷ 1,000 = 0.8 kWh
  kWh/month = 0.8 × 30 = 24 kWh
  Monthly $ = 24 × $0.15 = $3.60
  Annual $  = $3.60 × 12 = $43.20
```

> **Why 30 days?** The calculator uses a standard 30-day month. For a more precise figure on a specific month, replace 30 with the exact number of days.

---

## Reference Table — Typical Appliance Power & Monthly Costs

Costs calculated at the 2024 U.S. average residential rate of **$0.1621/kWh** (EIA). Assumes hours/day shown.

| Appliance | Typical Wattage | Avg. Hours/Day | kWh/Month | Est. Monthly Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Central AC (3-ton) | 3,500 W | 8 h | 840 kWh | $136.16 |
| Electric water heater | 4,000 W | 3 h | 360 kWh | $58.36 |
| Space heater (1,500 W) | 1,500 W | 6 h | 270 kWh | $43.77 |
| Clothes dryer | 5,000 W | 1 h | 150 kWh | $24.32 |
| Refrigerator (new) | 150 W | 24 h | 108 kWh | $17.51 |
| Desktop PC + monitor | 300 W | 8 h | 72 kWh | $11.67 |
| 55" LED TV | 120 W | 5 h | 18 kWh | $2.92 |
| LED bulb (60 W equiv.) | 9 W | 5 h | 1.35 kWh | $0.22 |
| Phone charger | 5 W | 2 h | 0.3 kWh | $0.05 |
| Game console (standby) | 13 W | 24 h | 9.36 kWh | $1.52 |

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Window AC unit (summer billing shock)
A 1,000 W window AC runs 10 hours/day throughout June:
- kWh/month = (1,000 × 10 × 30) ÷ 1,000 = **300 kWh**
- At $0.1621/kWh → **$48.63 added to the June bill**
- Running it only 7 hours/day instead saves ~$10.22/month with no special equipment.

### Case 2 — Home office energy audit
A remote worker runs: desktop PC (300 W), two monitors (60 W each), and a laser printer on standby (8 W) for 9 h/day:
- Total draw: 428 W
- kWh/month = (428 × 9 × 30) ÷ 1,000 = **115.56 kWh**
- At $0.1621/kWh → **$18.74/month** ($224.88/year)
- Switching to a 45 W laptop + one monitor (40 W) = 85 W → $3.72/month → saves **$15/month**

### Case 3 — EV home charging cost
A Level 2 home charger (7,200 W) charges an EV for 1.5 hours per night:
- kWh/month = (7,200 × 1.5 × 30) ÷ 1,000 = **324 kWh**
- At $0.1621/kWh → **$52.52/month** added to the electricity bill ($630.24/year)
- Many utilities offer off-peak EV rates (~$0.09/kWh), cutting that to **$29.16/month**

---

## Common Errors

1. **Confusing Watts with kilowatts.** The formula divides by 1,000 because 1 kW = 1,000 W. Forgetting this step makes every answer 1,000× too large. Always express wattage in Watts and divide by 1,000 to get kWh.

2. **Using the wrong electricity rate.** The national average (≈$0.1621/kWh) hides huge regional variation — Hawaii averages $0.3940/kWh while Louisiana averages $0.0994/kWh (EIA, 2024). Always check your actual utility bill or your state's average for accurate results.

3. **Ignoring standby / phantom load.** Many devices draw power 24/7 even when "off": game consoles (~13 W), cable boxes (~17 W), routers (~10 W). Over a year, phantom loads can add up to 100–200 kWh — roughly $16–$32 — per household. Measure with a Kill-A-Watt meter if unsure of the true draw.

4. **Assuming rated wattage equals actual wattage.** A 5,000 W dryer doesn't run at full power the entire cycle; a 1,500 W microwave might only run at 70% duty cycle. For precision, use a smart plug energy monitor to measure actual consumption rather than the nameplate rating.

5. **Not accounting for seasonal variation.** An AC or electric heater's effective "hours per day" varies dramatically by month. Calculating an annual figure from a fixed 8 h/day assumption for a seasonal appliance overstates or understates true annual cost; run separate calculations per season.

---

## Related Calculators

Explore these other tools to build a complete picture of your home energy spending:

faq.q: What is a kilowatt-hour (kWh) and how does it relate to Watts?
faq.a: A Watt (W) is a unit of power — the rate at which energy is used at any instant. A kilowatt-hour (kWh) is a unit of energy — it equals 1,000 Watts running for one full hour. So a 100 W light bulb running for 10 hours consumes exactly 1 kWh. Your utility bill charges you per kWh consumed, not per Watt. The conversion is: kWh = (Watts × Hours) ÷ 1,000.

faq.q: What is the average electricity rate in the United States in 2024?
faq.a: According to the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA), the average U.S. residential electricity rate in 2024 is approximately $0.1621 per kWh. However, this varies enormously by state: Hawaii is the most expensive at ~$0.3940/kWh, while Louisiana is among the cheapest at ~$0.0994/kWh. Always check your own monthly utility bill — look for the 'rate' or 'price per kWh' line — for the most accurate calculation.

faq.q: How much does it cost to run a refrigerator for a month?
faq.a: A modern ENERGY STAR-certified refrigerator typically draws 100–200 W but cycles on and off, averaging about 1–2 kWh per day. At the national average rate of $0.1621/kWh, that's roughly $4.86–$9.73/month ($58–$117/year). Older refrigerators (pre-2000) can draw 400–600 W continuously, costing two to three times as much — making refrigerator upgrades one of the highest-ROI appliance investments.

faq.q: Does leaving devices plugged in when not in use really cost money?
faq.a: Yes — this is called 'standby power' or 'phantom load.' The Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory estimates that standby power accounts for roughly 5–10% of a typical U.S. household's electricity consumption, averaging about $100/year. Common culprits include cable/satellite boxes (~17 W), gaming consoles (~13 W), and older desktop PCs (~10 W on standby). Using smart power strips or unplugging devices when not in use can meaningfully reduce this waste.

faq.q: How many kWh does a typical American home use per month?
faq.a: According to the EIA's 2022 Residential Energy Consumption Survey, the average U.S. home consumed 899 kWh per month (10,791 kWh/year). This figure varies significantly by region: homes in the South average about 1,142 kWh/month due to heavy air conditioning use, while Northeast homes average around 568 kWh/month. Your home's size, insulation quality, climate zone, and appliance efficiency all drive this number.

faq.q: Is the 30-day assumption accurate, or should I use the exact number of days in the month?
faq.a: Using 30 days is a standard approximation that gives results within ±3.3% for any real month (ranging from 28 to 31 days). For a January calculation, use 31; for February, use 28 or 29. The formula becomes: kWh = (Watts × Hours/day × Days in month) ÷ 1,000. For annual cost projections, multiplying the 30-day result by 12 is accurate enough for budgeting purposes and matches how most utilities bill.

faq.q: How do I find the wattage of an appliance if it's not labeled?
faq.a: Most appliances display rated wattage on a label near the power cord, on the back panel, or in the owner's manual. If the label shows amps (A) and volts (V) instead, use Ohm's Law: Watts = Amps × Volts (standard U.S. household voltage is 120 V; large appliances like dryers use 240 V). For the most accurate real-world measurement — since rated wattage is the maximum, not the average draw — use a plug-in energy monitor (e.g., Kill A Watt meter, ~$25) to measure actual consumption over time.

faq.q: What's the fastest way to reduce my electricity bill using this calculator?
faq.a: First, identify your highest-wattage, longest-running appliances — typically HVAC systems, water heaters, and dryers together account for over 50% of home energy use (EIA data). Run each through the calculator and prioritize the biggest cost drivers. Reducing thermostat setpoints by 7–10°F for 8 hours/day can cut HVAC costs by up to 10% (DOE), and switching to ENERGY STAR appliances can reduce device-level consumption by 10–50% depending on the category.

faq.q: Does solar panel generation affect how I should use this calculator?
faq.a: Yes. If you have rooftop solar, the net cost of running a device depends on whether you're drawing from the grid or consuming your own generation. During peak solar hours, your effective rate may be $0/kWh (self-consumed solar) or even negative if your utility pays a feed-in tariff. Outside those hours, grid rates apply. Use this calculator to understand gross consumption in kWh, then compare against your system's production data (from your inverter app) to calculate the true net grid cost.
